article.title: Does Employment Slow Cognitive Decline? Evidence from Labor Market Shocks

summary: New causal evidence from U.S. data shows that negative local labor demand shocks reduce employment among men aged 51-64 and accelerate cognitive decline. This suggests that continued employment into older ages may help delay dementia and cognitive impairment, highlighting the cognitive health benefits of working beyond traditional retirement age.
keyPoints: Study uses Health and Retirement Study (HRS) data combined with a Bartik instrument to isolate exogenous labor demand shocks.
Finds significant cognitive score declines linked to reduced employment caused by negative local labor market conditions.
Effects are strongest for men aged 51 to 64, whose employment is more sensitive to local labor demand than women or older men.
Supports the hypothesis that working longer can slow age-related cognitive decline, beyond correlations seen near retirement age.
Reflects growing public health concerns as dementia prevalence rises with increased life expectancy.
Findings have implications for labor and health policy, suggesting incentives to maintain workforce participation among older adults may yield cognitive health benefits.

rewrite.title: Employment's Role in Slowing Cognitive Decline
rewrite.body:
As life expectancy increases, cognitive decline and dementia have become more prevalent, posing significant health challenges. This study leverages data from the U.S. Health and Retirement Study (HRS) and employs a Bartik instrument to exploit exogenous shifts in local labor demand, providing causal evidence on how employment impacts cognitive health. The analysis reveals that negative labor demand shocks, which reduce employment opportunities, correspond with substantial declines in cognitive function over time.

The effects are particularly pronounced among men aged 51 to 64, a group whose employment outcomes are more tightly linked to local labor market conditions compared to women or older men. This demographic sensitivity underscores the importance of employment during the pre-retirement years in maintaining cognitive health. Unlike prior research focused narrowly on the retirement age window, these findings suggest that continuing to work beyond traditional retirement ages can delay the progression of cognitive decline.

These results carry important implications for public health and labor policies. As dementia-related disability rises alongside longer lifespans, encouraging sustained workforce participation among older adults could serve as a protective factor against cognitive deterioration. Policymakers might consider labor market interventions or incentives that support employment continuity in later life to help mitigate the societal and economic burdens of age-related cognitive impairments.

Overall, the study provides robust evidence linking labor market dynamics to cognitive aging, emphasizing the potential cognitive benefits of working longer in an aging population.
